programs.name: Big Sky Waiver Program
programs.tagline: Get nursing-home-level care services at home if your parent meets Montana's strict income and medical needs requirements.

localEntities.name: Mountain Pacific Quality Health Foundation for referrals/reviews
County OPA offices

programs.intro:
If your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, Montana's Big Sky Waiver can pay for personal assistance, adult day health, homemaker services, medical equipment, and home modifications. Your parent must qualify for Medicaid first, then pass a clinical assessment proving they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management.

The program currently has a waiting list. Income must be below $967/month and assets below $2,000 (2025 limits). Because this waiver requires unmet needs that only these specific services can address, your parent must need at least one waiver service monthly that isn't available through regular Medicaid.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing home-level care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities of daily living) confirmed through clinical interview
otherRequirements: Must be financially eligible for Medicaid
Must have unmet needs that only Big Sky Waiver services can address
Must need at least one waiver service monthly

applicationGuide.summary: Call Mountain Pacific for referral screening, then apply for Medicaid at your county office — processing time not specified and there's currently a waiting list.

steps.title: Call for referral screening
steps.description: Contact Mountain Pacific Quality Health Foundation at (800) 219-7035 to discuss your parent's needs and get referred for assessment.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid eligibility
steps.description: Visit your county Office of Public Assistance to apply for Medicaid with income and asset documentation.

steps.title: Complete clinical assessment
steps.description: Schedule the Nursing Facility Level of Care interview (can be done in-person or by phone) to prove your parent needs nursing home-level care.

applicationGuide.tip: Start the Medicaid application first — you can't get Big Sky Waiver services without Medicaid eligibility.

categories.name: Clinical Assessment
items: Medical records showing need for daily care assistance

contentSections.text: The Big Sky Waiver currently has a waiting list. Even if your parent qualifies, there may be a delay before services begin.
For married couples, if only one spouse applies, both spouses' assets are still counted as jointly owned — but the non-applying spouse's income is disregarded.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for Big Sky Waiver?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from the $2,000 asset limit. However, if your family has significant assets, you may need Medicaid planning to meet the financial requirements.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over $967/month?
faqs.answer: He won't qualify. The income limit is strict at $967/month for 2025. If he's married and only he is applying, his spouse's income doesn't count — but both their assets do.

faqs.question: How long is the waiting list for Big Sky Waiver services?
faqs.answer: The state doesn't specify wait times. Contact Mountain Pacific at (800) 219-7035 for current information about the waiting list in your area.

faqs.question: What's the difference between regular Medicaid and Big Sky Waiver?
faqs.answer: Regular Medicaid covers medical care. Big Sky Waiver adds home and community services like personal assistance, homemaker services, adult day health, and home modifications — but only if your parent needs nursing home-level care.

faqs.question: Can Mom get Big Sky Waiver services while living in assisted living?
faqs.answer: Possibly, through the Adult Residential Living service. The program is designed to help people avoid nursing homes, so assisted living with waiver services may be an option depending on her needs.

faqs.question: What happens if Mom no longer needs nursing home-level care?
faqs.answer: She would lose Big Sky Waiver eligibility since the program requires ongoing nursing facility level of care. She'd keep regular Medicaid if still financially eligible.

programs.name: Community First Choice Services (CFCS) / Personal Care Services (PCS)
programs.tagline: Medicaid can pay for someone to help your parent with bathing, dressing, and daily tasks at home.

localEntities.name: Provider Type 12 enrollment via Optum/Conduent; examples: Summit Independent Living for self-directed

programs.intro:
If your parent needs help with bathing, dressing, or taking medication but wants to stay at home, Montana Medicaid can pay for a personal care aide through Community First Choice Services (CFCS) or Personal Care Services (PCS). Your parent can choose to direct their own care — meaning they can hire and manage their aide, including paying certain family members like adult children.

CFCS is the more comprehensive program, covering 95% of enrollees and including extras like emergency response systems and help with yard hazards. Everyone approved for the basic PCS program is automatically reviewed for CFCS upgrade. Because this requires Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment showing your parent needs significant daily assistance), most people who need this level of help will qualify.

exemptAssets: Primary home (if intent to return or spouse/disabled child lives there)

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Health condition that limits ability to perform Activities of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, medication management). CFCS requires Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment.
otherRequirements: Montana resident
Must be able to direct care or have representative
Pass Mountain Pacific Quality Health nurse assessment

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Medicaid office for initial screening, then complete a nurse assessment — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Contact Medicaid office
steps.description: Call your local Medicaid office or visit medicaid.mt.gov to start the screening process for CFCS/PCS eligibility.

steps.title: Complete nurse assessment
steps.description: Mountain Pacific Quality Health (MPQH) will send a nurse to assess your parent's functional needs and determine program eligibility.

steps.title: Get health care authorization
steps.description: Your parent's doctor must provide authorization showing medical necessity for personal care assistance.

steps.title: Choose service model
steps.description: Select either self-directed care (like Summit Independent Living) where you manage the aide, or agency-based care where an agency handles scheduling and management.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is already on Medicaid, the eligibility process focuses on the functional assessment rather than financial review.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary home (if intent to return or spouse/disabled child lives there)
Life insurance with face value under $1,500

tiers.name: Personal Care Services (PCS)
tiers.coverage: Medically necessary assistance with Activities of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, medication management)

tiers.name: Community First Choice Services (CFCS)
tiers.coverage: All PCS services plus Personal Emergency Response System, community integration support, yard hazard removal, correspondence assistance

categories.name: Financial
items: Property tax statements or home deed

categories.name: Medical
items: Health care professional authorization for personal care
Documentation of Activities of Daily Living needs

contentSections.text: Adult children can be paid caregivers through the self-directed option, but spouses, parents, and legal guardians cannot receive payment for providing care.

faqs.question: Can I get paid to care for my parent through this program?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you're an adult child and choose the self-directed care option. However, spouses, parents, and legal guardians cannot be paid caregivers. You'll need to enroll with a self-directed provider like Summit Independent Living to manage the paperwork and payments.

faqs.question: What's the difference between PCS and CFCS?
faqs.answer: PCS covers basic personal care assistance. CFCS includes everything in PCS plus extras like emergency response systems and yard hazard removal. Everyone approved for PCS is automatically reviewed for CFCS — 95% of people end up in the enhanced CFCS program.

faqs.question: Will Medicaid take my parent's house after they die?
faqs.answer: Montana may pursue estate recovery against the home after death to recoup Medicaid costs. However, recovery is delayed if a spouse, minor child, or disabled adult child lives in the home.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the $967 limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify if they have medical expenses that reduce their countable income, or through other Medicaid pathways. Contact your local Medicaid office to explore options — the income calculation can be complex.

faqs.question: How many hours of care can my parent get?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify hour limits. The Mountain Pacific Quality Health nurse assessment will determine the appropriate level of care based on your parent's specific functional needs.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their assets in a trust?
faqs.answer: Asset limits are $2,000 for a single person, $3,000 for couples. Certain trusts may protect assets, but Medicaid has strict rules about asset transfers. Consult an elder law attorney before moving assets.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs nursing-home-level care but wants to stay home, PACE provides all medical care, medications, and support services at no cost per visit.
programs.intro: If your parent is 55+ and needs significant daily care (help with bathing, dressing, medications, or memory issues), PACE might cover everything — doctor visits, medications, physical therapy, adult day care, and even transportation to appointments. Because PACE coordinates all care through one team, your parent won't juggle multiple doctors or insurance approvals. The program has no income or asset limits, but it's only available in select Montana communities. Once enrolled, your parent never pays deductibles, copays, or out-of-pocket costs for any PACE-covered service or medication.

summary: Must live in PACE service area
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified as needing nursing facility level of care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or supervision due to cognitive issues)
otherRequirements: Must live in a PACE service area
Must be able to live safely in the community with PACE services
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage (Part C) or Medicare prescription drug plans
Cannot be receiving hospice services

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local PACE program directly for an in-home assessment — processing time varies by program.

steps.title: Find your local PACE program
steps.description: Use Medicare's Find a PACE Plan tool or check the National PACE Association directory to see if PACE operates in your parent's area

steps.title: Contact the program directly
steps.description: Call your local PACE organization to discuss your parent's needs and schedule an initial consultation

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: A nurse will conduct an in-home assessment to determine if your parent meets nursing facility level of care requirements

steps.title: Get state certification
steps.description: Montana must certify that your parent qualifies for nursing-home-level care before enrollment can proceed

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has Medicare Advantage, they'll need to disenroll before joining PACE — plan this timing carefully to avoid coverage gaps.

contentSections.text: PACE is not available statewide in Montana. You must verify that a PACE program operates in your parent's specific service area before pursuing eligibility. Even if your parent meets all other requirements, they cannot enroll without local program availability.

About 90% of PACE participants qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid, which makes the program completely free. If your parent has Medicare but not Medicaid, they'll pay a monthly premium for the Medicaid-covered services.

faqs.question: What if my parent has Medicare Advantage — can they still get PACE?
faqs.answer: No, your parent must disenroll from Medicare Advantage (Part C) before joining PACE. They also can't have a Medicare prescription drug plan. PACE becomes their complete Medicare and Medicaid coverage.

faqs.question: How do I know if my parent needs 'nursing facility level of care'?
faqs.answer: This generally means your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, medication management, or supervision due to dementia or cognitive issues. A PACE nurse will do an in-home assessment to determine if your parent meets Montana's specific criteria.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for Medicaid but they need PACE services?
faqs.answer: PACE has no income limits. If your parent doesn't qualify for Medicaid, they can still join PACE but will pay a monthly premium for the Medicaid-covered portion of services. Medicare-covered services remain free.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their current doctors if they join PACE?
faqs.answer: No, PACE uses its own team of doctors, nurses, and specialists. Because PACE coordinates all care, your parent will get new providers within the PACE program. This is how they eliminate the need for referrals and insurance approvals.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent moves to a different city in Montana?
faqs.answer: If your parent moves outside their PACE service area, they'll lose PACE coverage and need to find new healthcare arrangements. PACE coverage is tied to specific geographic areas, not the entire state.

faqs.question: How quickly can my parent start receiving PACE services?
faqs.answer: Timeline varies by local program. After the initial contact, your parent needs an in-home functional assessment and state certification for nursing facility level of care. Contact your local PACE program for their specific enrollment timeline.

programs.name: Medicaid for the Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 65+, blind, or disabled, this covers medical care, prescriptions, and long-term care with little to no out-of-pocket costs.

localEntities.name: Local DPHHS Human and Community Services Division offices statewide

programs.intro: If your parent is 65 or older, blind, or disabled and has limited income and assets, ABD Medicaid covers their medical care, prescription drugs, vision care, and long-term services with minimal out-of-pocket costs. Because this follows strict SSI income rules, your parent's monthly income must stay below approximately $1,215 for a single person — significantly lower than regular Medicaid. If your parent already receives SSI benefits, they automatically qualify for ABD Medicaid without additional income testing. The program also covers nursing home care and home-based services through waivers if your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management (called Nursing Facility Level of Care).

summary: Monthly income around $1,215 or less (single person)
Montana resident and U.S. citizen/qualified alien

exemptAssets: Primary home (if spouse, child under 21, or blind/disabled child lives there)

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For long-term care services: Must need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management (Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment required)
otherRequirements: Must be Montana resident
U.S. citizen or qualified alien
Must be aged (65+), blind, or disabled per Social Security Administration criteria

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at apply.mt.gov or contact your local DPHHS office — processing time not specified in state materials.

steps.title: Check if your parent gets SSI benefits
steps.description: If they receive SSI, they automatically qualify for ABD Medicaid without additional income testing. You'll still need to apply, but the process is streamlined.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of identity, Montana residency, income (pay stubs, SSI award letters), asset statements, and medical documentation of age/blindness/disability.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at apply.mt.gov using the general Medicaid application, or visit your local DPHHS Human and Community Services office.

steps.title: Complete medical assessment if needed
steps.description: For long-term care services, your parent will need a functional assessment to determine if they meet Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements.

applicationGuide.tip: If your parent might qualify for SSI but hasn't applied yet, apply for SSI first — it makes ABD Medicaid eligibility automatic and often provides additional monthly income.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary home (if spouse, child under 21, or blind/disabled child lives there)
Burial funds up to SSI limits
Retirement accounts (for Medicaid for Workers with Disabilities variant only)

tiers.name: Regular ABD Medicaid
tiers.description: For those meeting strict SSI income standards
tiers.incomeLimit: Around $1,215/month for single person (follows SSI standards)
tiers.coverage: Full Medicaid benefits with little to no out-of-pocket costs

tiers.name: Medically Needy
tiers.description: If income exceeds regular limits but you have high medical expenses
tiers.incomeLimit: Above regular limits with medical spenddown
tiers.coverage: Full Medicaid benefits after meeting spenddown amount

tiers.name: Medicaid for Workers with Disabilities (MWD)
tiers.coverage: Full Medicaid benefits with possible cost-sharing fees

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of Montana residency (utility bill, lease)
Social Security card or immigration documents

categories.name: Financial
items: Pay stubs or SSI award letters

categories.name: Medical
items: Social Security Administration disability determination (if applicable)
Medical records documenting blindness or disability
Functional assessment documents (for long-term care services)

contentSections.text: If your parent already receives SSI benefits, they automatically qualify for ABD Medicaid — many families miss this connection and don't realize they're eligible for comprehensive medical coverage.

ABD Medicaid has much stricter income limits than regular Montana Medicaid expansion. Your parent's home equity cannot exceed $752,000 unless a spouse, child under 21, or blind/disabled child lives there.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for ABD Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you, a spouse, a child under 21, or a blind/disabled child lives in the home. Otherwise, the home equity cannot exceed $752,000 in 2026. The home is always protected while your parent lives there.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the ABD limit?
faqs.answer: He may qualify for Medically Needy ABD Medicaid by 'spending down' his excess income on medical expenses each month. Alternatively, if he's disabled and works, Medicaid for Workers with Disabilities allows income up to $3,261/month for a single person.

faqs.question: Does ABD Medicaid cover nursing home care?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your parent meets Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements — meaning they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management. A clinical assessment determines this eligibility.

faqs.question: If Mom gets SSI, does she automatically get ABD Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, SSI recipients automatically qualify for ABD Medicaid without additional income testing. You still need to apply, but the income eligibility is already established through SSI.

faqs.question: Can ABD Medicaid help keep Dad at home instead of a nursing home?
faqs.answer: Yes, through home and community-based waiver programs that can provide services like home modifications, personal care, and adult day programs — but your parent must still meet the same Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements.

faqs.question: What happens to Dad's Medicare if he gets ABD Medicaid?
faqs.answer: ABD Medicaid works alongside Medicare for those who have both. Medicaid can help pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, and cover services Medicare doesn't, like long-term care and some dental services.

programs.name: Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), Qualifying Individual (QI)
programs.tagline: If your parent's Medicare premiums and copays are straining their budget, these programs can pay those costs entirely.

localEntities.name: Local DPHS/OPA offices in each Montana county/tribe; find via dphhs.mt.gov.

programs.intro:
If your parent is on Medicare with limited income, Montana's Medicare Savings Programs can eliminate their Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays. The highest tier (QMB) covers all Medicare Part A and B out-of-pocket costs — meaning your parent pays $0 for Medicare-covered doctor visits, hospital stays, and medical equipment. Even the middle tiers (SLMB and QI) eliminate the $185/month Medicare Part B premium, plus automatically qualify your parent for Extra Help with prescription drug costs.

Three income tiers determine coverage level. QMB provides the most comprehensive coverage for individuals earning up to $1,305/month, while QI covers Medicare Part B premiums for those earning up to $1,761/month. Because Montana follows federal asset limits ($9,950 for individuals, $14,910 for couples), your parent's savings and retirement accounts will be reviewed — but their home, one car, and burial plots don't count.

All three programs automatically enroll your parent in Medicare Part D Extra Help, reducing prescription costs to $1-$4 per medication. Unlike some programs, providers cannot legally bill your parent for Medicare cost-sharing if they have QMB coverage.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premiums ($2,220/year) plus potential deductibles and coinsurance

otherRequirements: Must be eligible for Medicare Part A
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through your local Montana DPHS office by phone, mail, or in-person — processing takes up to 45 days.

steps.title: Find your local office
steps.description: Visit dphhs.mt.gov to locate your county DPHS office or call 1-800-362-1504 for assistance.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect Medicare card, income statements, bank statements, and ID before applying.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online through Montana Access Portal, by phone, mail, or in-person at your local office.

steps.title: Wait for determination
steps.description: QMB coverage begins the first of the month after approval. SLMB and QI can be retroactive up to 3 months.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Up to 45 days for QMB, effective first of month after all info received. SLMB/QI effective first of application month, retroactive up to 3 months prior.
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI may have federal funding limits and close enrollment if funds are exhausted
applicationGuide.tip: Apply even if income seems slightly over — Montana excludes SNAP benefits from income and applies a $20 disregard that might qualify your parent.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part A premiums, Part B premiums, deductibles, and all coinsurance. Providers cannot bill you for Medicare cost-sharing.

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premiums ($185/month). Plus automatic Extra Help for prescriptions.

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premiums ($185/month). Plus automatic Extra Help for prescriptions.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Real estate other than primary home

categories.name: Medicare & Identity
items: Medicare card or Medicare eligibility letter

categories.name: Financial Records
items: Property tax records for any real estate

contentSections.text: QI has federal funding limits and may close enrollment if funds are exhausted. SLMB and QI can provide retroactive coverage up to 3 months, but QMB cannot — apply early if your parent's income qualifies.

If your parent has QMB, providers cannot legally bill them for Medicare deductibles or coinsurance. If you receive unexpected bills, contact the provider and reference your parent's QMB coverage — this is a federal protection.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. Montana doesn't count the primary home toward the asset limit, regardless of its value. Your parent can own their home and still qualify.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: Apply anyway. Montana excludes SNAP benefits from income calculations and applies a $20 general income disregard. Annual cost-of-living adjustments are also disregarded for QMB to prevent loss of eligibility.

faqs.question: Does this affect Dad's Medicare coverage or doctors?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent keeps all their current Medicare benefits and can see the same doctors. This program just pays the out-of-pocket costs — premiums, deductibles, and coinsurance.

faqs.question: Will Montana take my parent's house after they die?
faqs.answer: No. Estate recovery does not apply to Medicare Savings Program benefits. Montana cannot recover these premium payments from your parent's estate.

faqs.question: How much could this actually save?
faqs.answer: At minimum, $2,220/year in Medicare Part B premiums for all three tiers. QMB can save $8,000+ annually by covering the Medicare Part A deductible ($1,676), Part B deductible ($240), and all copays for doctor visits and medical services.

faqs.question: What happens to prescription drug coverage?
faqs.answer: All three programs automatically qualify your parent for Medicare Part D Extra Help, reducing prescription costs to $1-$4 per medication instead of standard copays that can be $50-$100+ per prescription.

programs.name: SNAP (Food Stamps)
programs.tagline: Monthly grocery money for parents 60+ on fixed incomes — typically $100-300/month on an EBT card.

localEntities.name: Local DPHHS Human and Community Services Division offices across Montana

programs.intro:
If your parent is struggling to afford groceries on a fixed income, SNAP provides monthly benefits loaded onto an EBT card they can use at most grocery stores. The amount depends on their household size and income after deductions — typically $100-300/month for seniors. Montana has expanded eligibility for households with someone 60 or older, making it easier to qualify than in many other states.

The program works by calculating your parent's net income after allowing deductions for housing costs, medical expenses over $35/month, and other qualifying expenses. Because medical deductions can significantly lower their counted income, many seniors qualify even if their gross income seems too high. Benefits are deposited monthly on an EBT card that works like a debit card for food purchases.
programs.savingsSource: Monthly benefits vary by household net income

summary: Assets under $4,500 if income over 200% poverty level
otherRequirements: U.S. citizen or qualifying immigration status
Social Security number for all applying household members
Must live with and buy/prepare food with spouse and children under 22

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at apply.mt.gov — typically takes 30 days, or 7 days if your parent has very low income.

steps.title: Gather income and expense documents
steps.description: Collect pay stubs, Social Security statements, rent/mortgage receipts, and medical bills over $35/month for the past month.

steps.title: Complete SNAP application
steps.description: Apply online at apply.mt.gov or download forms from dphhs.mt.gov/HCSD/SNAP. Include all household members who live together and buy/prepare food together.

steps.title: Submit to local DPHHS office
steps.description: Submit application and documents online, by mail, or in person to your local Human and Community Services Division office.

steps.title: Complete interview
steps.description: DPHHS will schedule a phone or in-person interview to review your parent's application and verify information.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Typically 30 days; expedited within 7 days if very low income/no resources
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent has medical expenses over $35/month, include all documentation — this deduction can significantly lower their counted income and increase benefits.

contentSections.heading: Gross Income Limits by Household Size
contentSections.footnote: October 2025 - September 2026 limits for households with member 60+ or disabled

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Social Security numbers for all household members

categories.name: Financial
items: Pay stubs or Social Security statements

contentSections.text: If your parent's income seems too high, they may still qualify through the net income test after deductions for housing costs and medical expenses over $35/month. Medical deductions can significantly lower their counted income.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house and car if they get SNAP?
faqs.answer: Yes. The home your parent lives in and all vehicles are exempt from the asset limit. Only cash, bank accounts, and non-exempt property count toward the $4,500 asset limit.

faqs.question: What if my parent's Social Security puts them just over the income limit?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify through Montana's net income test. After deducting housing costs, medical expenses over $35/month, and other allowable expenses, their counted income may be low enough to qualify.

faqs.question: Do I have to include my parent's spouse in the application?
faqs.answer: Yes. Spouses who live together must apply as one household, even if they keep separate finances. The same rule applies to children under 22 living with parents.

faqs.question: What medical expenses count as deductions?
faqs.answer: For people 60+ or disabled, medical expenses over $35/month can be deducted from income. This includes insurance premiums, prescription costs, medical equipment, and transportation to medical appointments.

faqs.question: Can my parent use SNAP benefits to buy any food?
faqs.answer: SNAP covers most food for home preparation, including meat, dairy, bread, fruits, and vegetables. It cannot be used for alcohol, tobacco, hot prepared foods, or non-food items like soap or vitamins.

faqs.question: How long do SNAP benefits last?
faqs.answer: Benefits are ongoing but require periodic recertification. Your parent will need to reapply every 12-24 months and report any significant changes in income or household composition.

programs.name: Montana Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Help paying heating bills for parents on fixed incomes — direct payments to utility companies.

localEntities.name: Action for Eastern Montana (serves eastern Montana)
Montana Department of Public Health and Human Services (DPHHS) - statewide administration
Local eligibility offices throughout Montana

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to pay heating bills during Montana winters, LIHEAP provides one-time payments made directly to their utility company. This federal program helps cover heating costs for households that meet income limits — $33,719/year for a single person, $44,095 for a couple.

Because LIHEAP pays the utility company directly, your parent doesn't handle the money — it simply reduces what they owe on their heating bill. The program also offers crisis assistance if their heater breaks or they receive a shutoff notice. Parents who already receive SNAP benefits are automatically eligible and get priority processing.

summary: Must pay heating costs (renters or homeowners)
All household members must be included
otherRequirements: Must pay for heating costs (renters or homeowners eligible)
All people living in the home must be included on application
Cannot have dependents living out-of-state

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at apply.mt.gov or call 1-800-551-3191 if your parent is 60+ or has disabilities.

steps.title: Gather documents
steps.description: Collect recent fuel bill, income records for all household members 18+, and Social Security numbers for everyone in the home.

steps.title: Complete application
steps.description: Apply online at apply.mt.gov or get paper application from local Office of Public Assistance. All household members 18+ must sign.

steps.title: Submit during open season
steps.description: Applications only accepted October 1 – April 30. Apply as early as possible since funding is limited.

steps.title: Payment goes to utility
steps.description: If approved, LIHEAP pays your parent's utility company directly — money never goes to your parent.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Funding is limited; some local agencies may stop accepting applications earlier if funds run out
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent receives SNAP, they're automatically eligible and get priority — mention this on the application.

contentSections.text: Applications only accepted October 1 – April 30 each year. Apply early — some offices stop accepting applications when funding runs out.
If your parent gets SNAP benefits, they automatically qualify for LIHEAP and receive priority processing. Make sure to mention SNAP on the application.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if they rent and don't pay the utility bill directly?
faqs.answer: Yes, renters are eligible as long as they pay heating costs — either directly to the utility or as part of their rent. You'll need the landlord's contact information for the application.

faqs.question: What counts as income for the application?
faqs.answer: All gross monthly income (before taxes) for everyone in the household age 18+. This includes wages, Social Security, unemployment, child support, retirement benefits, alimony, and self-employment income.

faqs.question: Can Mom get help if her heater breaks in the middle of winter?
faqs.answer: Yes, Crisis LIHEAP provides emergency assistance for broken heaters, utility shutoff notices, or running out of heating fuel. This is separate from regular LIHEAP and available for immediate energy emergencies.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is just over the limit?
faqs.answer: They won't qualify for regular LIHEAP, but may still be eligible for Crisis LIHEAP in emergencies. Also check if they qualify for weatherization assistance, which has higher income limits (200% of poverty level).

faqs.question: Does LIHEAP help with air conditioning costs in summer?
faqs.answer: No, Montana LIHEAP only covers heating costs, not cooling. The program runs October through April and focuses on winter heating assistance only.

faqs.question: Can my parent apply if they live with roommates?
faqs.answer: If roommates share the same utility bill, they're all counted as one household for LIHEAP purposes, even if they don't share other expenses. Everyone on the utility bill must be included in the application.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.tagline: Free home energy efficiency improvements if your parent qualifies for energy assistance programs.

localEntities.name: Local Eligibility Offices (statewide list via dphhs.mt.gov); Tribal LIHEAP Offices; e.g., Action for Eastern Montana (Glendive), Human Resource Council (406-728-3710), CAPNM, Action Inc. Butte.[1][4][6][7][8]

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles with high heating bills, Montana's Weatherization Assistance Program provides free home energy improvements like insulation, air sealing, and heating system repairs. Because this program is tied to LIHEAP (energy assistance), your parent must first qualify for energy help — typically households earning at or below 60% of state median income.

The program serves both homeowners and renters, though rental properties require landlord permission. Your parent automatically qualifies if they receive SNAP, SSI, or TANF. Because demand exceeds capacity, approved applicants are ranked by priority — elderly and disabled households get preference, but there's no guaranteed timeline for service.

summary: Must qualify for LIHEAP energy assistance first
Income at or below 60% state median
Auto-eligible with SNAP, SSI, or TANF
otherRequirements: Must be LIHEAP eligible or qualify for 'Weatherization Only' up to 200% Federal Poverty Level
Home must not have been previously weatherized
U.S. citizen, qualified alien, or lawful permanent resident
For rentals: landlord must sign access agreement

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through your local eligibility office using the combined LIHEAP/Weatherization application — processing time varies by priority level.

steps.title: Find your local office
steps.description: Contact your regional sub-grantee office (like Action for Eastern Montana or Human Resource Council) or visit dphhs.mt.gov for the statewide list.

steps.title: Complete combined application
steps.description: Fill out the LIHEAP and Weatherization application together — you need both programs.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Bring proof of income, citizenship status, Social Security numbers, and a recent fuel/heating bill.

steps.title: Submit during application period
steps.description: Applications accepted October 1 through April 30, though local offices may accept year-round.

steps.title: Wait for priority ranking
steps.description: If approved, you'll be ranked by priority level based on income, energy burden, and vulnerable household members.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — depends on priority ranking and available funding
applicationGuide.waitlist: Priority-based ranking system — elderly and disabled households get preference, but no guaranteed timeline
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent receives SNAP, SSI, or TANF, mention this upfront — they're automatically income-eligible.

tiers.name: LIHEAP Eligible
tiers.incomeLimit: At or below 60% of state median income
tiers.coverage: Full weatherization services with highest priority for elderly/disabled households

tiers.name: Weatherization Only
tiers.description: For households slightly over LIHEAP limits
tiers.coverage: Same weatherization services but lower priority ranking

offices.name: Action Inc.
offices.address: Butte (serves Beaverhead, Deer Lodge, Granite, Madison, Powell, Silver Bow counties)

categories.name: Identity
items: Social Security numbers for all household members
Proof of U.S. citizenship or qualified alien status

categories.name: Housing
items: For rentals: Landlord-signed access agreement (form DPHHS-EAP-013)

contentSections.text: Even if approved, you'll be placed on a priority waitlist. If not served within a year, you must reapply. Elderly and disabled households get priority, but there's no guaranteed timeline for service.
Homes that were previously weatherized cannot receive services again. If you're unsure about your parent's home history, ask during the application process.

faqs.question: Can my parent get weatherization if they rent their home?
faqs.answer: Yes, renters are eligible, but the landlord must sign an access agreement (form DPHHS-EAP-013) giving permission for the work. The landlord cannot charge rent increases for one year after weatherization.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is slightly over the LIHEAP limit?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify under 'Weatherization Only' if her income is at or below 200% of the Federal Poverty Level. However, she'll have lower priority than LIHEAP-eligible households.

faqs.question: How long does the weatherization work take once they're approved?
faqs.answer: The timeline isn't specified and depends on your priority ranking and available contractors. If approved but not served within one year, you must reapply.

faqs.question: What exactly do they do to the house?
faqs.answer: Services include insulation, air sealing, heating system repairs or replacements, and other energy efficiency upgrades. They also provide energy education. The specific work depends on a home assessment.

faqs.question: Can Dad apply if his house was weatherized years ago?
faqs.answer: No, homes that have previously received weatherization assistance are not eligible for the program again, regardless of how long ago it was done.

faqs.question: Does being on Social Security help with priority?
faqs.answer: If your parent receives SSI (Supplemental Security Income), they're automatically income-eligible. Regular Social Security counts toward income limits. Being elderly gives priority ranking once approved.

programs.name: State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.tagline: Free Medicare guidance to help your parent choose the right plans and avoid costly mistakes.

localEntities.name: Administered by Montana Office on Aging via Area Agencies on Aging statewide; local examples include Roosevelt County SHIP Counselors and Richland County Council on Aging

programs.intro:
If your parent has Medicare and you're confused by plan options, enrollment deadlines, or billing issues, Montana's SHIP program provides free one-on-one counseling to cut through the complexity. SHIP counselors help compare Part D prescription plans, explain Medigap options, assist with Medicare enrollment, and help your parent apply for programs that reduce Medicare costs like Extra Help or Medicare Savings Programs.

Because SHIP counselors don't sell insurance, they provide objective advice focused on your parent's specific needs. They'll come to your parent's home if they're homebound, and there are no income limits or eligibility requirements beyond having Medicare or being a family caregiver.

otherRequirements: Must be Medicare beneficiary, family member, or caregiver seeking Medicare-related counseling

applicationGuide.summary: Call the statewide number and you'll be connected immediately to a local counselor in your area.

steps.title: Call the statewide SHIP line
steps.description: Call 1-800-551-3191. This connects you to your local Area Agency on Aging counselor based on your location.

steps.title: Schedule counseling session
steps.description: The local counselor will schedule a phone session, in-person meeting, or home visit based on your parent's needs.

steps.title: Gather Medicare information
steps.description: Have your parent's Medicare card, current insurance details, prescription list, and income information ready if seeking help with low-income programs.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate phone connection with local counselor; appointment scheduling varies by local availability
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is homebound, specifically request a home visit when you call — this service is available but you need to ask for it.

contentSections.text: SHIP counselors don't sell insurance and aren't affiliated with insurance companies, so their advice is completely objective and focused on your parent's needs.

faqs.question: Can SHIP help if my parent already has Medicare and just wants to switch plans?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP counselors specialize in comparing Part D prescription plans and Medicare Advantage options during open enrollment periods. They'll review your parent's current medications and costs to find better coverage.

faqs.question: What if my parent needs help paying for Medicare premiums and deductibles?
faqs.answer: SHIP counselors help apply for programs that reduce Medicare costs, including Medicare Savings Programs, Extra Help for prescription costs, and Medicaid if your parent qualifies based on income.

faqs.question: Will a counselor come to my parent's home if they can't travel?
faqs.answer: Yes. Home visits are available for homebound individuals. Request this when you call 1-800-551-3191 and the local counselor will arrange a home visit.

faqs.question: Can SHIP help with Medicare billing problems or suspected fraud?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP counselors help resolve Medicare billing issues and assist with reporting suspected Medicare fraud, waste, or abuse to protect your parent from scams.

faqs.question: Is there any cost for SHIP counseling services?
faqs.answer: No. All SHIP counseling is completely free, including home visits. There are no income limits or fees for any services.

faqs.question: Can I get help even if my parent isn't on Medicare yet but will be soon?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP helps with Medicare eligibility questions and enrollment guidance for people approaching Medicare age or those under 65 with disabilities who qualify for Medicare.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels
programs.tagline: Hot meals delivered to your parent's door if they're 60+ and can't prepare food safely.

localEntities.name: Cascade County: 406-454-6993, Great Falls
Western MT Area VI Agency: programs@area6aging.org, local senior centers
Mom's Meals for Medicaid waivers statewide via case managers

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggles to cook or shop for groceries due to mobility issues, illness, or disability, Meals on Wheels delivers hot, nutritious meals directly to their home. Your parent gets one meal per day, five days a week (Monday through Friday), delivered between 10am and 12:30pm by volunteers who also provide an informal wellness check.

Each meal meets half of the recommended daily nutrition requirements and follows low-salt, low-fat guidelines. The suggested donation is around $4.50 per meal, but this is based on what your parent can afford — income is not a barrier to getting meals. If your parent has Medicaid waivers or certain Medicare Advantage plans, meals may be completely free.

Meals on Wheels operates locally, not statewide. Your parent must live within a participating area's delivery zone and be assessed as homebound or unable to prepare meals safely.

summary: Homebound or unable to cook safely
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be homebound or unable to cook/prepare meals due to disability, mobility issues, or health conditions (assessed during home visit)
otherRequirements: Must reside within program delivery zone
Spouse or dependents may also qualify in some programs

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local Area Agency on Aging for a home assessment — service can start within a week in most areas.

steps.title: Call your local provider
steps.description: Contact your Area Agency on Aging or senior center to request meals. In Great Falls/Cascade County, call 406-454-6993. In western Montana, email programs@area6aging.org.

steps.title: Schedule home assessment
steps.description: A staff member will visit your parent's home to assess their age, health, mobility, meal preparation ability, and dietary needs. They'll also collect emergency contact information.

steps.title: Start meal delivery
steps.description: If approved, meals begin delivery Monday through Friday between 10am-12:30pm. Your parent commits to receiving meals 5 days per week.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Within a week in most areas, longer if there's a waitlist
applicationGuide.waitlist: Some programs have waitlists that can extend processing time
applicationGuide.tip: You, your parent's doctor, or a social worker can make the referral — your parent doesn't have to call themselves.

categories.name: Identity & Age
items: Proof of age (driver's license, birth certificate)
Proof of residence in delivery area

categories.name: Health Information
items: Medical documentation of disability or health condition (if applicable)
List of dietary restrictions or special needs

contentSections.text: Meals on Wheels is not available everywhere in Montana. You must confirm your parent lives within a local program's delivery zone before applying. If no service exists in their area, ask about alternatives.
If your parent has Medicaid waivers, check with their case manager about Mom's Meals — this separate program provides free home-delivered meals statewide and may have more availability.

faqs.question: What if my parent can still drive but has trouble cooking?
faqs.answer: Your parent may still qualify if they have health conditions, mobility issues, or disabilities that make meal preparation unsafe, even if they can leave home occasionally. The home assessment will determine eligibility based on their specific situation.

faqs.question: Can my parent choose which meals they receive?
faqs.answer: Meals are pre-planned to meet nutritional guidelines, but dietary restrictions and special needs are accommodated. During the assessment, staff will discuss your parent's preferences and medical dietary requirements.

faqs.question: What happens on weekends and holidays?
faqs.answer: Most Meals on Wheels programs only deliver Monday through Friday. Your parent will need other arrangements for weekend and holiday meals. Some programs may provide extra meals on Fridays to cover weekends.

faqs.question: What if my parent can't afford the suggested donation?
faqs.answer: The donation is truly suggested — your parent cannot be denied meals for inability to pay. The amount is adjusted based on what they can afford, and those with Medicaid waivers often get meals completely free.

faqs.question: Can meals be left if no one is home?
faqs.answer: No, meals require someone to be home to receive them since volunteers also provide a wellness check. If your parent will be away, they need to notify the program in advance to pause delivery.

faqs.question: How long can my parent receive meals?
faqs.answer: There's no time limit for long-term need. However, if your parent's condition improves and they can prepare meals again, the program encourages transitioning to congregate meals at senior centers for continued social interaction.

programs.name: Respite Care (via Big Sky Waiver)
programs.tagline: Up to 30 days annually of temporary caregiver relief when your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home.

localEntities.name: Mountain Pacific (statewide coordinator)
County OPA offices

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management but wants to stay home, Montana's Big Sky Waiver can provide up to 30 days annually of respite care. This gives you temporary relief while ensuring your parent receives professional care in their home, your home, or community settings.

Because this program requires nursing home-level care qualification, your parent must already be eligible for Montana Medicaid and need significant daily assistance. The waiver covers comprehensive home and community-based services beyond just respite — including personal assistance, homemaker services, and adult day health — all coordinated through one case management team.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment confirming your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities that would otherwise require nursing home care)
otherRequirements: Montana Medicaid enrollment required
Must use at least one waiver service monthly
Unmet care needs that justify waiver services

applicationGuide.summary: Call Mountain Pacific for referral — processing time not specified, and waitlists are likely due to limited program slots.

steps.title: Confirm Medicaid eligibility
steps.description: Contact your county Office of Public Assistance to verify your parent's Montana Medicaid enrollment. If not enrolled, apply for Medicaid first.

steps.title: Request waiver referral
steps.description: Call Mountain Pacific at (800) 219-7035 to request Big Sky Waiver assessment and explain your parent's care needs.

steps.title: Complete nursing facility assessment
steps.description: Schedule the required Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment (done by phone or in-person) to document your parent's daily care needs.

steps.title: Wait for slot availability
steps.description: If approved, you may be placed on a waitlist until a waiver slot becomes available in your area.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Likely due to waiver caps on enrollment slots
applicationGuide.tip: Start the Medicaid application process early — you cannot access Big Sky Waiver services without active Medicaid enrollment.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts for Medicaid
included: Real estate other than primary home
excluded: Burial funds up to certain limits

categories.name: Medicaid Proof
items: Montana Medicaid card or approval letter

categories.name: Medical Documentation
items: Recent medical records showing functional limitations
Documentation of daily care needs (bathing, dressing, medication management)

contentSections.text: Waitlists are common. Even if your parent qualifies, you may wait months for an available waiver slot. Apply as soon as you anticipate needing services.
The 30-day annual respite limit can be extended case-by-case for health and safety needs. Discuss your specific situation with your case manager if you need more coverage.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for the Big Sky Waiver?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's primary home is exempt from Medicaid asset limits and won't be counted toward the $2,000 asset limit. This is true whether they own it outright or are still paying a mortgage.

faqs.question: What if my parent needs more than 30 days of respite care per year?
faqs.answer: The base limit is 30 days annually, but this can be extended without variance for health and safety needs. Work with your case manager to document why additional respite days are necessary for your parent's wellbeing.

faqs.question: Does my parent have to be in a nursing home to qualify for this program?
faqs.answer: No. The Nursing Facility Level of Care assessment determines if your parent needs the same level of daily care provided in nursing homes — but the whole point of the waiver is to provide that care at home instead.

faqs.question: Can we choose where the respite care happens?
faqs.answer: Yes. Respite care can be delivered in your parent's home, your family home, the care provider's home, or community settings. You'll work with your case manager to determine the best option.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income is slightly over the Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: Contact your county Office of Public Assistance to discuss Medicaid spend-down options or other pathways to eligibility. Income limits follow standard Montana Medicaid rules, which can be complex with various exemptions.

faqs.question: Is respite care the only service we can get through Big Sky Waiver?
faqs.answer: No. Big Sky Waiver includes personal assistance, homemaker services, adult day health, nursing, therapies, and other services. Your case management team will create a comprehensive care plan based on your parent's specific needs.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.tagline: If your parent is 55+ and struggling to find work, this program provides paid community service jobs at $7.25/hour while helping them transition to permanent employment.

localEntities.name: Easterseals-Goodwill Northern Rocky Mountain (primary sub-grantee for MT)
Montana DLI Workforce Services Division (oversight)
Local Job Service Montana offices

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55 or older and unemployed or underemployed, SCSEP provides paid work experience at community organizations while helping them find permanent employment. Because this is transitional employment, your parent earns wages (minimum wage for average 20 hours per week) while gaining skills and work experience at nonprofits, schools, hospitals, or senior centers.

The program lasts 6-12 months and includes training in resume writing, interviewing, computer skills, and job placement assistance. Your parent must have income at or below 125% of the federal poverty level. Priority goes to veterans, those over 65, people with disabilities, and rural residents.

Because slots are federally limited, there may be waitlists in your area. The program is administered statewide through Easterseals-Goodwill Northern Rocky Mountain, with referrals through local Job Service Montana offices.

summary: Income at or below 125% federal poverty level
otherRequirements: Unemployed or underemployed
Must have U.S. work authorization
Priority for veterans, those over 65, people with disabilities, low literacy, limited English, rural residents, homeless or at risk

applicationGuide.summary: Contact Easterseals-Goodwill directly or call the national helpline — processing time varies by local availability and waitlists.

steps.title: Check current income limits
steps.description: Verify your parent's income is at or below 125% of federal poverty level using current HHS guidelines (updated annually).

steps.title: Contact the program
steps.description: Call Easterseals-Goodwill Northern Rocky Mountain at their SCSEP line or visit esgw.org/scsep.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Prepare proof of age, income verification, and work authorization. Include veteran status or disability documentation if applicable for priority.

steps.title: Complete eligibility screening
steps.description: Program staff will verify eligibility and determine priority status. If slots are full, you may be placed on a waitlist.

steps.title: Get assigned to worksite
steps.description: Once accepted, your parent will be assigned to a community service worksite before training begins.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified — varies by local availability
applicationGuide.waitlist: Likely due to limited federal slots — varies by region and provider capacity
applicationGuide.tip: Emphasize any priority criteria your parent meets (veteran status, over 65, rural residence) when applying.

categories.name: Financial
items: Income verification (pay stubs, benefit statements, tax returns)
Documentation showing income at or below 125% federal poverty level

categories.name: Priority Status (if applicable)
items: Veteran discharge papers (DD-214) for veteran priority

contentSections.text: Federal funding limits the number of SCSEP slots available. Even if your parent qualifies, they may be placed on a waitlist until a position opens up.
This is transitional employment, not a permanent job. The goal is to help your parent find unsubsidized employment in the private sector within 6-12 months.

faqs.question: How much will my parent earn through SCSEP?
faqs.answer: Your parent will earn the highest of federal, state, or local minimum wage for an average of 20 hours per week. In Montana, this means at least $7.25 per hour.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income is just over the 125% poverty limit?
faqs.answer: They won't qualify for SCSEP, but they'll be referred to Job Service Montana for other employment assistance programs.

faqs.question: Can my parent choose where they work?
faqs.answer: No. SCSEP assigns participants to community service worksites like schools, hospitals, senior centers, or other nonprofits based on availability and your parent's skills.

faqs.question: What if there's no opening when my parent applies?
faqs.answer: They'll likely be placed on a waitlist. Priority goes to veterans, those over 65, people with disabilities, and other specified groups, so emphasize any qualifying criteria.

faqs.question: Will this affect my parent's Social Security or other benefits?
faqs.answer: SCSEP wages may affect some benefits since this is actual employment. Check with Social Security or the benefits administrator before enrolling.

faqs.question: What happens after the 6-12 month program ends?
faqs.answer: The goal is job placement assistance to help your parent find permanent, unsubsidized employment. The program provides ongoing support during the transition.

programs.name: Legal Services for Seniors
programs.tagline: Free legal help for Montana seniors 60+ to create wills, powers of attorney, and other essential estate planning documents.

localEntities.name: Montana Department of Public Health and Human Services (DPHHS) Office on Aging
Montana Legal Services Association (MLSA)
Montana Aging Services Bureau clinics
Area Agencies on Aging (local via Eldercare Locator 800-677-1116)[4][7][8]

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and needs basic legal documents like a will or power of attorney, Montana provides free legal services specifically for seniors. This program offers free legal advice clinics, telephone consultations, and help drafting essential estate planning documents including wills, powers of attorney, living wills, and transfer deeds.

Because these services are designed for low-income seniors, your parent will go through income and asset screening on a case-by-case basis — there are no fixed dollar limits. The program covers basic estate planning needs but does not handle complex estates, large assets, or trusts. Services are available statewide through annual clinics and year-round through Montana Legal Services Association.

otherRequirements: Montana resident
Low-income status (determined case-by-case)
Civil legal issues related to aging or estate planning
U.S. citizenship or eligible immigration status (for some providers)

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at montanalawhelp.org or call 1-800-666-6899 — expect a callback in 3-5 business days.

steps.title: Choose your application method
steps.description: Apply online at montanalawhelp.org/apply-legal-services (takes 10-15 minutes) or call MLSA Helpline at 1-800-666-6899 (Tuesday-Thursday 9am-1pm MT)

steps.title: Complete income screening
steps.description: Provide information about your parent's household income, assets, and legal problem details. No specific dollar limits — each case is reviewed individually

steps.title: Wait for callback or attend clinic
steps.description: MLSA will call back within 3-5 business days, or attend one of the annual statewide estate planning clinics (check DPHHS website for schedule)

steps.title: Receive legal assistance
steps.description: Get help drafting documents, legal advice, or referrals to other legal organizations based on your parent's needs

applicationGuide.processingTime: Expect callback in 3-5 business days; urgent deadlines noted on application get priority
applicationGuide.waitlist: High call volume may cause delays; keep trying phone or use online form
applicationGuide.tip: If you can't get through by phone, use the online application form — it often processes faster during busy periods.

contentSections.text: This program only covers basic estate planning documents. If your parent has complex assets, large estates, or needs trust services, you'll need to hire a private attorney.
High phone volume at MLSA can make it hard to get through. Use the online application form at montanalawhelp.org if you can't reach them by phone.

faqs.question: What documents can my parent get help with?
faqs.answer: The program helps with basic estate planning documents: wills, powers of attorney (financial and healthcare), living wills, transfer on death deeds, and declarations of homestead. These are simple statutory forms that work for most basic situations.

faqs.question: What if my parent's estate is complicated or valuable?
faqs.answer: This program only handles basic estate planning with simple statutory forms. If your parent has complex assets, large estates, or needs trusts, the program will refer you to private attorneys who can handle more sophisticated planning.

faqs.question: How do they determine if my parent qualifies financially?
faqs.answer: There are no fixed income or asset limits. Montana Legal Services Association screens each case individually based on household income, assets, and the type of legal problem. Low-income seniors get priority.

faqs.question: Can my parent get help if they're under 60?
faqs.answer: No, this specific program is only for Montana residents age 60+. If your parent is younger, they can still apply to Montana Legal Services Association directly or contact the State Bar for referrals.

faqs.question: Where can my parent get help in person?
faqs.answer: Montana holds annual estate planning clinics statewide with local networks of attorneys, notaries, and volunteers. Check the DPHHS website for clinic schedules and locations, or call your local Area Agency on Aging.

faqs.question: What if my parent has an urgent legal deadline?
faqs.answer: Note urgent deadlines on your application — these get priority processing. You can also call the Legal Services Developer Program at 1-800-332-2272 for urgent matters.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.tagline: Free advocacy when your parent faces problems in their nursing home or assisted living facility.

localEntities.name: State office in Helena; local via Area Agencies on Aging (e.g., Western Montana Area VI Agency, Flathead County Agency on Aging). Directory: https://dphhs.mt.gov/sltc/aging/longtermcareombudsman/.

programs.intro:
If your parent lives in a nursing home, assisted living facility, or hospital swing bed in Montana and faces problems with their care, the Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program provides free advocacy. Trained ombudsmen investigate complaints about poor care, billing issues, resident rights violations, or improper discharge threats.

Over 30 certified ombudsmen across Montana handle everything from medication errors to financial exploitation. Because ombudsmen are independent advocates (not employed by facilities), they can push back against administrators and care teams on your parent's behalf. All services are confidential and ombudsmen only act with your parent's permission.

The program serves any resident in certified long-term care facilities statewide. No income limits, no paperwork, no waiting period — just call when problems arise.

summary: Resident of nursing home, assisted living, or hospital swing bed
Family can call on resident's behalf
otherRequirements: Must be resident of certified long-term care facility in Montana
Or family member/friend calling on behalf of resident

applicationGuide.summary: Call 1-800-332-2272 and ombudsmen respond immediately to investigate your complaint.

steps.title: Call the complaint line
steps.description: Call 1-800-332-2272 during business hours or contact your local ombudsman through Area Agencies on Aging.

steps.title: Describe the problem
steps.description: Explain the care issue, billing problem, or rights violation. No forms required — provide details verbally.

steps.title: Ombudsman investigates
steps.description: Certified ombudsman visits the facility, interviews staff and residents, and works to resolve the complaint confidentially.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate response to complaints; ombudsmen move into action at resident's request
applicationGuide.tip: Keep notes about incidents (dates, staff names, what happened) to help the ombudsman investigate effectively.

contentSections.text: The ombudsman can only act with your parent's permission. If your parent has dementia or cannot make decisions, the ombudsman may still help but will need to work within legal guardianship arrangements.

faqs.question: Can I call even if my parent doesn't want me to?
faqs.answer: You can call to report concerns, but ombudsmen must get your parent's permission before taking action. If your parent has dementia or is incapacitated, the ombudsman will work within existing guardianship arrangements or help connect you to appropriate resources.

faqs.question: What if the facility retaliates against my parent for the complaint?
faqs.answer: Retaliation is illegal and ombudsmen specifically watch for this. Because they visit facilities regularly and maintain ongoing relationships, they can monitor your parent's treatment after complaints and take action if retaliation occurs.

faqs.question: How is this different from calling the health department?
faqs.answer: Ombudsmen focus on resident advocacy and work to resolve problems directly with facilities. Health departments do regulatory inspections and citations. Ombudsmen often resolve issues faster and with less disruption to your parent's daily life.

faqs.question: Can the ombudsman help with Medicare or Medicaid billing issues?
faqs.answer: Yes, ombudsmen investigate billing problems and can help resolve disputes over Medicare/Medicaid charges, improper billing practices, or benefits issues related to your parent's facility care.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives at home with a caregiver?
faqs.answer: This program only covers residents in certified facilities (nursing homes, assisted living, hospital swing beds). For home-based care issues, the ombudsman can refer you to other aging services or Adult Protective Services.

faqs.question: Is there help available after business hours?
faqs.answer: The ombudsman line operates during business hours only. For immediate safety emergencies, call 911. For urgent but non-emergency issues, leave a message and ombudsmen will respond the next business day.

stateOverview.intro: Montana offers 14 programs that can save your aging parent hundreds to thousands of dollars monthly — from $100-300 in monthly grocery money through SNAP to comprehensive medical care at no cost per visit through PACE. Nine of these are qualification-based benefits that provide direct financial help or services, while four are free resources available to any Montana senior regardless of income. Most income-based programs use similar thresholds, so if your parent qualifies for one, they likely qualify for several others. Start by checking the three highest-impact programs below, then explore additional help based on your parent's specific needs.

startHere.name: Medicaid for the Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD)
startHere.why: Covers medical care, prescriptions, and long-term care with little to no out-of-pocket costs if your parent is 65+ and meets income limits.

startHere.name: SNAP (Food Stamps)
startHere.why: Provides $100-300 monthly for groceries and has the most straightforward qualification process for seniors 60+.

startHere.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
startHere.why: Can eliminate Medicare premiums and copays entirely if your parent's Medicare costs are straining their budget.

startHere.name: Big Sky Waiver Program
startHere.why: Provides nursing-home-level care services at home if your parent meets strict medical and income requirements.

programs: Community First Choice Services (CFCS) / Personal Care Services (PCS)
Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
byNeed.description: These programs can pay for someone to help your parent with bathing, dressing, and daily tasks, or provide comprehensive medical care at home.

programs: Medicaid for the Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD)
Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB), Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB), Qualifying Individual (QI)
Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
byNeed.description: These programs cover medical expenses, Medicare premiums and copays, or provide all medical care at no cost per visit.
Monthly grocery money or hot meals delivered to your parent's door if they're 60+ and struggling with food preparation or costs.

byNeed.need: Help with utility bills and home repairs
programs: Montana Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
byNeed.description: Direct payments to utility companies for heating bills and free home energy efficiency improvements.
Temporary caregiver relief and employment opportunities for seniors who need income or want to stay active.
Free guidance on Medicare decisions, estate planning documents, and advocacy for nursing home or assisted living problems.

quickFacts: Most Montana benefit programs use similar income limits, so qualifying for one program often means qualifying for several others
Montana seniors 60+ have access to both statewide programs (like SNAP) and Montana-specific programs (like Big Sky Waiver)
Programs like SHIP, Legal Services for Seniors, and the Ombudsman Program are free to all Montana seniors regardless of income
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Nine Montana programs require your parent to meet specific income or medical criteria to receive direct financial benefits or services — like the Big Sky Waiver Program's nursing-home-level care at home or SNAP's monthly grocery money. Four programs are free resources available to any Montana senior: SHIP provides Medicare guidance, Legal Services helps with wills and estate planning, and the Ombudsman Program advocates for seniors in care facilities, all regardless of your parent's income or assets.
